stem: A 41-year-old woman is referred by her general practitioner to a community psychiatry clinic after 18 months of persistent abdominal pain and fatigue. She has had two normal colonoscopies, a normal gastroscopy, normal abdominal CT, normal coeliac serology, and a normal pelvic ultrasound across this period. She has consulted 11 different clinicians, frequently checks symptom-tracking apps and online forums for several hours each day, and reports that the uncertainty about what is wrong is so distressing that she has reduced her part-time bookkeeping work to one day per week. She describes the pain as a constant 6 out of 10, worsening with stress, and is convinced something serious has been missed. She denies low mood pervading her day, denies anhedonia, sleeps 6 hours nightly with early waking only when pain is severe, and her K-10 is 24. She uses paracetamol most days and has no other regular medications. She has never had clear hypomanic, psychotic, or panic episodes.

data_table.Examination: Anxious, preoccupied with bodily focus, organised thought, no psychosis. Mild epigastric tenderness, otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: K-10 24. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, coeliac serology, faecal calprotectin all within reference. Two prior colonoscopies and a gastroscopy normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 classification for this presentation?

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder with care-seeking behaviour
options.rationale: Illness anxiety disorder requires preoccupation with HAVING or acquiring a serious illness with minimal or absent somatic symptoms; her dominant feature is the symptoms themselves. Would be correct only with minimal somatic symptoms (Source: DSM-5).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with persistent course
options.rationale: DSM-5 SSD requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms PLUS disproportionate or persistent thoughts, anxiety, or behaviours about them, for more than 6 months. Her persistent pain, excessive health-related time use, functional decline, and disproportionate concern across 18 months meet criteria; persistent course is specified at >6 months of severe symptoms (DSM-5; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder with somatic predominance
options.rationale: GAD requires excessive worry across multiple domains for 6+ months with 3+ of restlessness/fatigue/concentration/irritability/muscle tension/sleep; somatic symptom focus is the discriminator. Would be correct in multi-domain worry pattern (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Functional neurological disorder, mixed sensory type
options.rationale: FND requires altered voluntary motor/sensory function with positive incompatibility signs (Hoover's, tremor entrainment); abdominal pain and fatigue do not fit. Would be correct with positive incompatibility signs (Source: DSM-5).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self
options.rationale: Factitious requires intentional falsification or induction of signs/symptoms with deceptive intent without obvious external reward; absent here. Would be correct with deception (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Somatic symptom disorder hinges on the response to symptoms (disproportionate thoughts, anxiety, time, behaviours) rather than whether symptoms are medically explained. DSM-5 moved away from 'medically unexplained' framing toward affirming the symptom and addressing the disproportionate cognitive-behavioural reaction (RANZCP; DSM-5).
key_points: SSD = >=1 distressing somatic symptom + disproportionate thoughts/anxiety/behaviours + >6 months
Illness anxiety disorder centres on feared disease with little or no somatic symptom
FND requires positive neurological incompatibility signs
Factitious disorder requires deceptive intent without external incentive
explanation.pearls: Do not require 'unexplained' symptoms for SSD - genuine medical conditions can coexist (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 29-year-old graphic designer presents to the emergency department with sudden onset right leg weakness that began 3 days ago after a minor work argument. She has no headache, no visual change, no bowel or bladder symptoms, and no sensory level. She walks with a dragging right leg that appears to swing in a circumduction not typical of pyramidal hemiparesis. Power in the right hip extensors is 2/5 when tested in isolation, but when she is asked to flex the left hip against resistance while lying supine, you feel strong downward pressure under her right heel. Right knee and ankle reflexes are normal and symmetric, plantar response is flexor, sensation is reported as patchy with a non-anatomical boundary at mid-thigh. Cranial nerves are intact. MRI brain and whole spine completed yesterday are normal. FBC, UEC, glucose, TSH, B12 and HIV serology are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% on air, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Hoover's sign positive on the right (involuntary hip extension during contralateral hip flexion against resistance). Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor, non-anatomical sensory boundary.
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and spine normal. FBC, UEC, glucose, TSH, B12, HIV serology normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat MRI brain with stroke protocol and start dual antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: Stroke is unlikely with normal MRI, symmetric reflexes, flexor plantar, positive Hoover's indicating functional weakness; antiplatelets in this setting harms without benefit. Would be correct in confirmed stroke (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Admit for high-dose IV methylprednisolone empirically for suspected multiple sclerosis
options.rationale: MS is excluded by normal MRI and positive functional sign; steroids would entrench illness behaviour. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Tell the patient the scan is normal and there is nothing wrong, with GP follow up in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Telling FND patients 'nothing is wrong' worsens distress, increases relapse and is associated with poorer outcomes; FND is a positive diagnosis to be communicated. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Explain the positive Hoover's sign as a rule-in diagnosis of functional neurological disorder and refer for FND-specific physiotherapy
options.rationale: FND is a positive diagnosis based on incompatibility signs (Hoover's, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness with normal MRI and reflexes). The first therapeutic step is a clear, non-stigmatising explanation that the diagnosis is made BY the positive sign, framed as a brain functioning problem that is treatable, followed by referral to FND-trained physiotherapy which is the strongest evidence-based intervention (RANZCP; FND Society Consensus; NICE NG217 stroke excluded).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Commence quetiapine 25 mg nocte for somatisation and arrange psychiatric admission
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for FND and inpatient psychiatric admission is rarely indicated; physiotherapy is the evidence-based primary treatment. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is a positive diagnosis. Hoover's sign (involuntary recovery of hip extension during contralateral hip flexion) is the bedside test that rules in functional leg weakness. Treatment hinges on a clear non-blaming explanation plus FND-specific physiotherapy (FND Society 2021).
key_points: FND positive signs: Hoover's, hip abductor sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness with normal reflexes
Communicate diagnosis as real, common, and treatable; avoid 'nothing wrong'
FND-specific physiotherapy has the strongest evidence base
Psychological therapy is adjunctive, not primary
explanation.pearls: If MRI is normal AND a positive sign is present, you have rule-in evidence - say so explicitly to the patient (FND Society).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

subtopic_detail: Illness anxiety disorder vs somatic symptom disorder
stem: A 38-year-old IT consultant attends his general practitioner for the fourth time in 8 weeks requesting another full blood count and tumour marker panel. He is convinced he has pancreatic cancer despite a normal abdominal CT, normal CA 19-9, normal LFTs, and normal pancreatic enzymes obtained 3 weeks ago. He has minor occasional epigastric flutter and mild fatigue but no weight loss, no jaundice, and no anorexia. He spends 3 to 4 hours daily on patient forums and oncology websites, has read three textbooks on pancreatic cancer staging, and asks colleagues to feel his abdomen for masses. His K-10 is 22. He acknowledges every reassurance gives him relief for around 24 hours before doubt returns. There is no family history of pancreatic cancer. He has no other regular medications. He sleeps 6 hours and his weight is stable. His functional decline at work is described as moderate.

data_table.Examination: Anxious, pressured speech around health topics, no thought disorder. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, lipase, CA 19-9 all within reference. Abdominal CT 3 weeks ago: normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 diagnosis?

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain
options.rationale: SSD requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms as focus; his somatic symptoms are minor and dominant feature is fear of having pancreatic cancer. Would be correct with predominant somatic symptoms (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder, severe
options.rationale: GAD requires worry across multiple domains for 6+ months; his worry is narrowly focused on single feared disease (illness anxiety pattern). Would be correct in multi-domain worry (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder, care-seeking type
options.rationale: DSM-5 illness anxiety disorder: preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness; somatic symptoms are absent or mild; high health-related anxiety; excessive health-related behaviours OR maladaptive avoidance; duration >=6 months; not better explained by another disorder. Repeated investigations, reassurance-seeking, and forum use mark the care-seeking type (DSM-5; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder with contamination obsessions
options.rationale: OCD requires obsessions and/or compulsions consuming over 1 hour daily; his behaviours are health-focused reassurance-seeking, not classical OCD. Would be correct with classical OCD obsessions (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Delusional disorder, somatic type
options.rationale: Delusional disorder somatic type requires fixed false beliefs of bodily disease with delusional intensity; he accepts reassurance partially. Would be correct with delusional intensity (Source: DSM-5).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

explanation.summary: Illness anxiety disorder is dominated by fear of having or acquiring a serious illness with minimal somatic symptoms; SSD is dominated by distressing somatic symptoms themselves. Discriminator: 'is the patient distressed by the symptom or by what the symptom means?' (DSM-5).
key_points: IAD: feared disease + minimal somatic symptoms + >6 months + health-related behaviours
Care-seeking type repeatedly investigates; care-avoidant type avoids medical contact
Reassurance provides only short-term relief, a hallmark feature
Differentiate from delusional disorder by reality testing
explanation.pearls: If reassurance helps for 24 hours then doubt returns, think illness anxiety, not delusion (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 45-year-old man presents to ED for the 7th time in 8 months with recurrent hypoglycaemia (plasma glucose 2.1-2.6 mmol/L, markedly elevated insulin and SUPPRESSED C-peptide each time). He has no diabetes, no insulinoma on prior CT and MRI. He is a former endocrinology nurse, currently unemployed, lives alone. He denies injecting insulin and becomes irritable when asked. He has no pending litigation, no Centrelink claim under reviewo active forensic process. Staff have observed him discarding small objects in the sharps bin after symptom onset. Each admission has involved invasive investigations and extended stays during which he is highly engaged with treating teams who describe him as 'the most knowledgeable patient we've ever had'. He receives no financial benefit from admissions but openly enjoys the medical-team attention. K-10 is 30 with profound loneliness.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 128/82, RR 16, BSL 2.3 mmol/L on arrival.
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, oriented, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Plasma glucose 2.3 mmol/L, insulin 480 pmol/L (markedly raised), C-peptide 90 pmol/L (suppressed), sulphonylurea screen negative. CT and MRI pancreas previously normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 classification of his behaviour?

options.text: Malingering for secondary financial gain
options.rationale: Malingering requires EXTERNAL incentive (financial, legal, evasion of duty, drug-seeking); he has no claim, litigation, forensic process. Would be correct with external incentive (Source: DSM-5).
source_refs: DSM-5-TR Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self
options.rationale: Three DSM-5 criteria are met: (i) falsification of physical signs (covertly self-administered insulin, evidenced by suppressed C-peptide with elevated insulin and observed sharps disposal); (ii) presentation as ill/injured/impaired; (iii) absence of obvious external incentive. The internal psychological gain (sick-role identity, relational connection with medical teams) is the discriminator from malingering and is consistent with his nursing-background insider-knowledge profile.

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain
options.rationale: SSD involves genuine somatic symptoms with disproportionate thoughts/feelings/behaviours; deliberate falsification takes case out of SSD. Would be correct with genuine symptoms (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)
options.rationale: Conversion requires neurological symptoms (weakness, tremor, sensory loss) without organic cause; biochemical hypoglycaemia is not neurological. Would be correct with neurological symptoms (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Delusional disorder, somatic type
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires fixed false belief; he is engaged in covert behaviour (sharps disposal) implying awareness of deception, inconsistent with delusional intensity. Would be correct with delusional intensity (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Factitious disorder vs malingering hinges on motivation: factitious is intentional symptom production motivated by the sick role with NO external incentive; malingering is intentional symptom production for external incentive (benefits, avoidance, drugs). Suppressed C-peptide with elevated insulin is a textbook signature of surreptitious insulin use (DSM-5; AMH).
key_points: Factitious disorder: intentional + deceptive + no external incentive (sick-role motive)
Malingering: intentional + deceptive + external incentive
Suppressed C-peptide + high insulin + negative sulphonylurea = exogenous insulin
Approach: avoid confrontation, address underlying psychological need, consult-liaison referral
explanation.pearls: Document objective evidence of induction (lab pattern, hidden items) before formulating - confrontation typically escalates the behaviour (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 52-year-old woman is referred to community mental health by her general practitioner for a 4-month history of fatigue, low mood, weight gain of 5 kg, constipation, cold intolerance, and reduced concentration. She has stopped her weekly bushwalking group and finds her brain feels foggy. Her sister has Hashimoto thyroiditis and her mother had pernicious anaemia. She has no prior psychiatric history, no suicidal ideation, and her affect is flat with mildly slowed thought tempo. She is not pregnant. She takes no regular medications and drinks 4 standard drinks weekly. Her general practitioner started sertraline 50 mg daily 6 weeks ago without baseline bloods, with no improvement, and has now sent her with K-10 of 28. On examination her skin is dry, reflexes show delayed relaxation phase, and there is a small diffuse non-tender goitre. HR is 52.

data_table.Examination: Slow speech, flat affect, dry skin, delayed reflex relaxation, small diffuse goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed by mental health team.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation before adjusting psychotropic therapy?

options.text: Switch sertraline to mirtazapine 30 mg nocte and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Switching antidepressants without first excluding medical mimic risks treating thyroid disease as treatment-resistant depression; AMH and RANZCP mandate exclusion of medical cause. Would never be correct without thyroid workup (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing syndrome
options.rationale: Cushing screening is for features suggestive of cortisol excess (central adiposity, striae, proximal myopathy, hypertension, hyperglycaemia); absent here. Would be correct with Cushing features (Source: APEG).

options.text: Brain MRI to exclude frontal lobe lesion
options.rationale: MRI not first-line with clear thyroid-pointing physical findings and no focal neurology; low pre-test probability. Would be correct with focal neuro findings (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: TSH, free T4 and thyroid peroxidase antibodies
options.rationale: Clinical features (bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, goitre, cold intolerance, weight gain, family history of autoimmune thyroid disease) point to primary hypothyroidism, most likely Hashimoto. TSH plus free T4 is the screening pair; TPO antibodies confirm autoimmune aetiology and guide monitoring of subclinical disease. RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines require excluding thyroid disease before labelling depression as primary (RANZCP; eTG).

options.text: Empirical levothyroxine 50 microgram daily without bloods
options.rationale: AMH requires confirmed biochemical hypothyroidism before levothyroxine to avoid iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Before labelling a depressive presentation as primary, screen for medical mimics with TSH at minimum. Bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, goitre, and an autoimmune family history make hypothyroidism a leading mimic and a treatable cause of mood and cognitive symptoms (RANZCP; eTG).
key_points: Baseline screen for mood presentations: TSH, FBC, UEC, LFTs, B12/folate, glucose, urine drug screen if indicated
Hypothyroid mimic features: bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, dry skin, goitre, cold intolerance
TPO antibodies confirm Hashimoto
Empirical levothyroxine without bloods risks thyrotoxicosis
explanation.pearls: Treatment-resistant depression with no baseline TSH is a diagnostic red flag, not a pharmacological problem (RANZCP).

stem: A 22-year-old previously well university student is admitted to a psychiatric inpatient unit with 3 weeks of insomnia, paranoid delusions and disorganised behaviour. Over 48 hours her presentation has evolved: she now has orofacial dyskinesias, brief episodes of unresponsiveness with eyelid twitching, autonomic instability (HR oscillating 140 then 48, BP 165/95 then 95/55), and low-grade fever 38.1. She has had NO antipsychotic exposure to date (admitted before any were given). Urine drug screen is negative. CK 320 U/L. No neck stiffness. Family report a brief flu-like illness 6 weeks ago. CT head normal. LP performed: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (35 cells/microL), normal glucose, mildly raised protein, no organisms on Gram stain, viral PCR (including HSV-1/2 and enterovirus) pending.

data_table.Vital signs: Fluctuating HR 48-140, BP 95/55 to 165/95, T 38.1, RR variable.
data_table.Examination: Orofacial dyskinesias, intermittent unresponsiveness with eyelid twitching, no neck stiffness, no focal deficit.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative. CK 320 U/L. CT head normal. CSF: WCC 35 (lymphocytic), protein 0.65 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (paired plasma 5.0).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigative AND empirical step in parallel?

options.text: Send serum AND CSF anti-NMDA receptor (and broader autoimmune encephalitis) antibodies, arrange MRI brain with contrast, request urgent neurology review, and start empirical IV aciclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
options.rationale: Multi-step: the prodromal flu-like illness, subacute psychiatric onset, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability and CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis form the classical anti-NMDAR encephalitis syndrome (Dalmau triad-plus). Definitive diagnosis requires BOTH serum and CSF antibodies (CSF more sensitive); MRI brain frequently normal but indicated for differential. While awaiting antibody result and HSV PCR, empirical aciclovir is standard because HSV encephalitis is treatable and the cost of delay is high. This combines confirmatory and empirical limbs concurrently.
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society for Immunology autoimmune encephalitis consensus

options.text: Increase olanzapine to 20 mg daily and add lorazepam for agitation while awaiting CSF PCR
options.rationale: Escalating antipsychotic in suspected autoimmune encephalitis worsens autonomic instability and dyskinesias, may precipitate NMS-overlap, and delays diagnosis. Would never be correct (Source: Titulaer 2013).

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir alone with no autoimmune antibody panel, on the assumption that lymphocytic pleocytosis is always HSV until proven otherwise
options.rationale: HSV cover is appropriate but failing to send autoimmune encephalitis antibodies delays diagnosis driving first-line immunotherapy. Would never be correct alone (Source: Titulaer 2013).

options.text: EEG and serial CK only, on the assumption this is neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS requires antipsychotic exposure; she has had none. CK is only mildly raised. EEG useful adjunct but not the priority. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Whole-body PET CT to exclude paraneoplastic origin as the first investigative step
options.rationale: Ovarian teratoma screening is ONCE anti-NMDAR antibodies confirm syndrome (then pelvic US/MRI first-line, not PET CT). Would be correct after antibody confirmation (Source: Titulaer 2013).

explanation.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the highest-yield psychiatric mimic to recognise in young women presenting with sub-acute psychosis plus movement disorder, autonomic instability, seizures, and lymphocytic CSF. Early antibody testing and immunotherapy improve outcomes; first-pass treatment as primary psychosis worsens prognosis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
key_points: Pentad: psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, seizures, autonomic instability, reduced consciousness
CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly raised protein
Test BOTH serum and CSF NMDAR antibodies (CSF is more sensitive)
Look for ovarian teratoma once antibody confirmed
Antipsychotics can paradoxically worsen presentation (NMS-like)
explanation.pearls: If a young woman with new psychosis has orofacial dyskinesias before any antipsychotic exposure, think NMDAR encephalitis (Lancet Neurology consensus).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: An 84-year-old woman with Alzheimer disease (baseline MoCA 16/30, lives at home with daily community nursing) is admitted with community-acquired pneumonia and treated with IV ceftriaxone plus oral azithromycin. On day 3 her family report a change from baseline: drowsy in the morning, agitated and pulling at her cannula by evening, sees small children in the curtains, and cannot identify where she is. Her usual mild forgetfulness has been replaced with fluctuating attention - cannot maintain a conversation >30 seconds. SpO2 95% on 2 L. No bowel motion in 4 days. Urinalysis: leucocytes only, no nitrites, asymptomatic. Recent additions to her chart include oxycodone 5 mg 4-hourly PRN (taken regularly), lorazepam 1 mg PRN nocte, and metoclopramide 10 mg TDS. CXR shows improving consolidation. CAM is positive for delirium.

data_table.Examination: Fluctuating attention, inattentive on months-of-year backwards, visual hallucinations, distractible.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP improving. Glucose 6.4 mmol/L. Urinalysis mild leukocytes only. CXR improving. ECG normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate INITIAL management plan, per the Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care delirium clinical care standard?

options.text: Add scheduled risperidone 1 mg BD for delirium agitation; continue oxycodone, lorazepam and metoclopramide unchanged at their current doses today
options.rationale: Adding antipsychotics without first deprescribing the precipitating drugs is wrong order; lorazepam and oxycodone are independent deliriogens. Would never be correct as initial step (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Start donepezil 5 mg daily for worsening Alzheimer disease and discharge home with primary care follow-up arranged at 4 weeks after this admission
options.rationale: Cholinesterase inhibitors are not delirium treatment; discharge of actively delirious patient with unsafe medications breaches standard. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Transfer to a closed psychiatric ward under an involuntary order for management of presumed psychosis arising from his underlying dementia process
options.rationale: Delirium is medical emergency managed on medical/geriatric ward; psychiatric ward transfer inappropriate and worsens outcome. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Deprescribe lorazepam, switch oxycodone to non-opioid analgesia, cease metoclopramide, address constipation and dehydration, optimise non-pharmacological care, do not treat asymptomatic pyuria
options.rationale: ACSQHC standard sequence: identify and remove precipitants first. Lorazepam, oxycodone, metoclopramide and constipation are all reversible contributors. Asymptomatic bacteriuria/pyuria in older adults should NOT be treated (eTG anti-pattern), and over-treatment of dipstick findings is a major contributor to inappropriate antibiotic use and further delirium. Antipsychotics are reserved for severe agitation when non-pharmacological measures fail.

options.text: Commence IV ciprofloxacin empirically for presumed urinary tract infection based on the urinalysis nitrites despite the absence of any urinary tract symptoms
options.rationale: Asymptomatic pyuria in 84-year-old should not be treated; ciprofloxacin adds delirium risk (CNS effects). Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC; Choosing Wisely).

explanation.summary: Hospital delirium superimposed on dementia is identified by acute change from baseline, fluctuating course, inattention, and altered cognition. The Australian Delirium Clinical Care Standard prioritises identifying and reversing precipitants over pharmacological sedation (Australian Commission).
key_points: DSM-5 delirium: acute change, fluctuation, inattention, additional cognitive disturbance, not better explained by dementia alone
First step: address deliriogenic medications, constipation, pain, hypoxia, hydration, sleep
Antipsychotics only for severe agitation with safety risk, lowest effective dose, short duration
Avoid treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in older adults
explanation.pearls: Opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, and metoclopramide are the highest-yield culprits to deprescribe (eTG).

sources.label: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care - Delirium Clinical Care Standard

stem: A 52-year-old man with recurrent major depressive disorder has not adequately responded to sertraline 200 mg daily for 10 weeks followed by venlafaxine titrated to 225 mg daily for 8 weeks (adequate dose, adequate duration, confirmed adherence by pharmacy records and partner observation). PHQ-9 has fallen from 22 to 19 only. He has no significant intolerance, no bipolar history, no psychotic features, is not catatonic, not refusing food, has no immediate suicide plan; K-10 today is 30. Sleep 5 hours, weight loss 3 kg over 8 weeks. Bloods: FBC normal, UEC normal (eGFR 92), TSH 1.8, fasting glucose 5.2, LFTs normal. ECG QTc 410 ms. He is a non-smoker, drinks 4 standard drinks weekly, no chronic medical illness. RANZCP defines TRD as failure of two adequate antidepressant trials. He is willing to take additional medication and to engage with augmentation but prefers to avoid ECT if alternatives exist.

data_table.Examination: Low mood, congruent affect, no psychosis, no catatonia.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 19. FBC, UEC (eGFR 92), TSH 1.8, fasting glucose 5.2, LFTs all within reference. ECG QTc 410 ms.
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine 225 mg daily (current), sertraline 200 mg daily for 10 weeks previously.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step per the RANZCP CPG TRD pathway?

options.text: Add lithium augmentation titrated to trough 0.4-0.8 mmol/L with renal and thyroid monitoring at baseline, 6 weeks and 3-monthly thereafter
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation has the strongest evidence base (multiple RCTs, NNT ~5) for SSRI/SNRI-resistant unipolar MDD and is the standard first augmentation in RANZCP TRD pathway. Target trough for augmentation is lower than mania prophylaxis (0.4-0.8 vs 0.6-1.0). The patient meets TRD criteria (two adequate trials) and has no contraindication (normal eGFR, normal TSH).

options.text: Switch venlafaxine to fluoxetine 20 mg daily monotherapy without augmentation
options.rationale: Switching to third antidepressant monotherapy has lower evidence than lithium or atypical-antipsychotic augmentation in TRD. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Add bupropion 150 mg daily as monoamine combination augmentation (not PBS-listed in Australia)
options.rationale: Bupropion combination has weaker evidence than lithium/aripiprazole/quetiapine augmentation and is not PBS-listed for depression in Australia. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg BD as anxiolytic augmentation for six weeks
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying augmentation in TRD and add dependence risk; not supported. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Refer urgently for ECT as the first-line treatment for resistant depression
options.rationale: ECT is for life-threatening (catatonia, food refusal, immediate suicide risk, severe psychotic depression) or after failure of multiple augmentations. Would be correct after augmentation failure (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: RANZCP defines TRD as failure of two adequate antidepressant trials. Lithium augmentation (target 0.4-0.8 mmol/L) and atypical antipsychotic augmentation (quetiapine or aripiprazole) are first-line; mirtazapine combination is reasonable; ECT is reserved for severe or rapid-response indications (RANZCP CPG 2020).
key_points: TRD = inadequate response to 2 adequate trials at therapeutic dose and duration
Lithium augmentation target 0.4-0.8 mmol/L (lower than mood stabilisation)
Baseline U+E, TSH, calcium, ECG before lithium; recheck level after 5-7 days
ECT indicated for catatonia, food refusal, high suicide risk, psychotic depression
Avoid benzodiazepines as augmentation
explanation.pearls: Lithium augmentation works within 2-4 weeks - re-evaluate at week 4, not week 12 (RANZCP).

stem: A 47-year-old woman with treatment-resistant MDD with atypical features has been on fluoxetine 40 mg daily for 9 months (partial response, PHQ-9 21 to 16). Her psychiatrist plans to switch to phenelzine, a non-selective irreversible MAOI. She has no concurrent serotonergic medications, no St John's Wort, no tramadol, no triptans, no pethidine, no linezolid, and is not pregnant. Renal and hepatic function are normal. Lives with supportive partner, no immediate suicide risk, agrees to weekly review during transition. She specifically asks why the wait between stopping fluoxetine and starting phenelzine is different to what her friend was told when switching from citalopram to phenelzine (2 weeks).

data_table.Examination: Mild psychomotor slowing, otherwise unremarkable, no autonomic instability.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all within reference. ECG normal.
data_table.Medications: Fluoxetine 40 mg daily for 9 months, no other serotonergic agents.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate switching strategy and the underlying reason?

options.text: Direct switch: stop fluoxetine today and commence phenelzine 15 mg TDS tomorrow without any washout interval despite norfluoxetine's long half-life
options.rationale: Direct switch from any serotonergic agent to MAOI risks fatal serotonin syndrome. Contraindicated. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Cross-taper: reduce fluoxetine while simultaneously titrating phenelzine over 2 weeks to maintain antidepressant cover despite serotonin syndrome risk
options.rationale: Overlapping serotonergic agents with irreversible MAOI is contraindicated; cross-tapers are for SSRI-to-SSRI/SNRI switches. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Stop fluoxetine and wait at least 5 weeks before commencing phenelzine because norfluoxetine has a half-life of ~7-15 days and requires ~5 half-lives for clearance
options.rationale: Fluoxetine's active metabolite norfluoxetine has a half-life of 7-15 days; ~5 elimination half-lives = ~35-75 days. The AMH and eTG specify a 5-week minimum washout from fluoxetine to MAOI. Other SSRIs require ~2 weeks (5 half-lives of citalopram/sertraline). This contrast is the AU-cutoff knowledge being tested.

options.text: Stop fluoxetine and wait only 5 days before starting phenelzine, ignoring the long active metabolite half-life of norfluoxetine in this washout
options.rationale: 5 days is grossly inadequate for fluoxetine because of norfluoxetine accumulation; 5-week wait required. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Continue fluoxetine and add low-dose phenelzine 7.5 mg daily as augmentation rather than switching agents entirely, accepting interaction risk
options.rationale: Co-administration of any SSRI with MAOI is contraindicated due to serotonin syndrome risk; no low-dose is safe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Switching from fluoxetine to a non-selective irreversible MAOI requires a 5-week washout because norfluoxetine has a half-life of 7-15 days. Other SSRI/SNRI to MAOI switches require 2 weeks; MAOI to another antidepressant requires 2 weeks for enzyme regeneration (AMH; eTG).
key_points: Fluoxetine to MAOI: 5-week washout (norfluoxetine half-life)
Other SSRI/SNRI to MAOI: 2-week washout
MAOI to another antidepressant: 2-week washout (enzyme regeneration)
Cross-taper and direct switch SSRI to MAOI are contraindicated
Watch for serotonergic drug interactions: tramadol, triptans, linezolid, St John's Wort, pethidine
explanation.pearls: Fluoxetine's washout rules dominate any switching plan involving an MAOI (AMH).

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome vs NMS vs anticholinergic discrimination
stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to ED by ambulance 6 hours after his community team added tramadol 100 mg QID for back pain. He was already on escitalopram 20 mg daily and started linezolid 4 days ago for a soft tissue infection. He is agitated and shivering with sweating; intermittent myoclonus is most prominent in the LOWER limbs; pupils are dilated (6 mm) and reactive; he is markedly hyperreflexic with sustained inducible clonus at the ankles, and ocular clonus is present. Skin is MOIST. HR 128, BP 168/92, RR 22, T 39.4. He is alert but agitated. CK 480 U/L. No recent antipsychotic exposure on review of his community team's chart. The ED registrar is choosing between the Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria, NMS criteria and an anticholinergic toxidrome.

data_table.Examination: Agitation, diaphoresis, dilated reactive pupils, lower-limb-predominant clonus, hyperreflexia, ocular clonus, moist skin.
data_table.Investigations: CK 480 U/L, FBC, UEC, glucose normal. Paracetamol level negative.
data_table.Medications: Escitalopram 20 mg daily, tramadol 100 mg QID (started today), linezolid for soft tissue infection.

lead_in: Which toxidrome best fits, and which is the most discriminating feature on examination?

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome - dry mucous membranes, absent bowel sounds, mydriasis and urinary retention are the deciding features in this drug-exposed patient
options.rationale: Anticholinergic has DRY skin/mucosae, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds; patient's skin is MOIST with hyperreflexia. Would be correct with anticholinergic features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome - lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, antipsychotic exposure and bradykinesia are the deciding features rather than clonus or hyperreflexia
options.rationale: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity, HYPOreflexia, autonomic instability, hyperthermia AND recent antipsychotic; patient has none of those. Would be correct in NMS phenotype (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Serotonin syndrome by Hunter criteria - the discriminating feature is spontaneous or inducible clonus with hyperreflexia in the setting of multi-serotonergic exposure
options.rationale: Hunter criteria require serotonergic agent PLUS one of: spontaneous clonus; inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; tremor + hyperreflexia; hypertonia + temperature >38 + ocular/inducible clonus. He meets multiple. Triple-serotonergic exposure: SSRI + tramadol (weak SNRI/opioid with serotonergic activity) + linezolid (non-selective MAOI). Distinct from NMS by hyperreflexia/clonus pattern and absence of antipsychotic exposure.

options.text: Sympathomimetic toxidrome from undisclosed stimulant intoxication - urine drug screen for amphetamines, cocaine or designer agents will confirm at presentation
options.rationale: Sympathomimetic shares tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, diaphoresis but lacks prominent neuromuscular features (hyperreflexia, clonus). Would be correct only with documented stimulant use (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Malignant hyperthermia from recent anaesthetic exposure - masseter rigidity and a rapidly rising end-tidal CO2 are the deciding features in this acute toxidrome
options.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia requires exposure to volatile anaesthetic or depolarising NMB; no exposure here. Would be correct only with anaesthetic exposure (Source: ANZCA).

explanation.summary: Three discriminators separate serotonin syndrome, NMS, and anticholinergic toxidrome: TIMING (SS hours; NMS days; anticholinergic hours), TONE (SS hyperreflexia + clonus; NMS lead-pipe rigidity + hyporeflexia; anticholinergic flaccid), and SKIN (SS and NMS sweaty; anticholinergic dry). Clonus, especially lower-limb and ocular, is the SS fingerprint (Hunter criteria; eTG).
key_points: SS: hours to onset, hyperreflexia, clonus (esp. lower limb + ocular), moist skin
NMS: days to onset, lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, moist skin, very high CK
Anticholinergic: hours to onset, dry skin/mucosae, mydriasis, urinary retention, NO clonus
Hunter criteria for SS require serotonergic exposure + one specific neuromuscular pattern
Treatment: stop all serotonergic drugs, cooling, IV benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine in severe cases
explanation.pearls: Linezolid is a hidden MAOI - combining with any serotonergic agent is high-risk (AMH).

stem: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder maintained on lithium carbonate 750 mg daily (trough level 0.7 mmol/L 6 weeks ago) presents to ED with 3 days of coarse tremor, ataxia, drowsiness, and vomiting. Over the past 2 weeks she has been treated for hypertension with newly added perindopril, has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for shoulder pain, and during a recent heatwave she has had reduced oral intake with no fluid replacement. She has lost 2 kg in 10 days. Her usual sertraline 50 mg daily has been unchanged. She is alert but confused, with hyperreflexia, coarse tremor, and gait ataxia. There is no clonus and no fever. Her ECG shows T-wave flattening with QTc 460 ms. Her current lithium level is 2.1 mmol/L. Creatinine has risen from baseline 75 to 145 micromol/L (eGFR fallen from 78 to 38).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 142/86, RR 16, T 36.8, mild dehydration.
data_table.Examination: Confused, coarse tremor, gait ataxia, hyperreflexia, no clonus, no rigidity, no fever.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 2.1 mmol/L, creatinine 145 (baseline 75), eGFR 38 (baseline 78), Na 138, K 4.2, ECG QTc 460 ms with T-wave flattening.
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily, perindopril (new 2 weeks), ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (recent), no diuretics.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lithium, give 1 L IV normal saline, recheck level in 24 hours
options.rationale: Lithium 2.1 with neurological features is moderate-to-severe toxicity; continuing risks progression to seizures, coma, irreversible nephrotoxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue lithium, withhold ACE inhibitor and ibuprofen, recheck level next week
options.rationale: Acute toxicity with neuro features and AKI requires immediate cessation and inpatient management; week delay risks fatal progression. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Give 50 g oral activated charcoal to bind unabsorbed lithium acutely
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium (an ion); ineffective. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease lithium and give IV frusemide 40 mg to enhance lithium excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretics WORSEN lithium toxicity (reduce volume, increase proximal tubular reabsorption); volume repletion with isotonic saline is correct. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease lithium, withdraw NSAID and ACE inhibitor, IV crystalloid, consult toxicology for haemodialysis
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and AMH require: cease lithium immediately, withdraw precipitants (NSAID, ACE inhibitor, dehydration are the classic three), give IV crystalloid to restore intravascular volume, and consider haemodialysis. Australian thresholds for dialysis: level >4.0 mmol/L irrespective of symptoms, OR level 2.5-4.0 with significant neurotoxicity (confusion, seizures, decreased consciousness) or renal impairment limiting clearance. He sits at 2.1 with AKI - imminent dialysis consult is appropriate, particularly with rising level given renal impairment (eTG Toxicology; AMH).

explanation.summary: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L maintenance, toxicity often >1.5 mmol/L). NSAID, ACE inhibitor/ARB, thiazide diuretic and dehydration are the four high-yield precipitants. Australian toxicology thresholds support dialysis at level >4.0 mmol/L, or 2.5-4.0 with significant neurotoxicity or impaired renal clearance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH).
key_points: Therapeutic range 0.6-0.8 (maintenance); toxicity threshold >1.5 mmol/L
Precipitants: NSAID, ACE-I/ARB, thiazide, dehydration, sodium depletion, AKI
Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium
Volume repletion with isotonic saline is first-line; avoid loop diuretics
Haemodialysis indicated >4.0 mmol/L, or 2.5-4.0 with neurotoxicity or renal failure
explanation.pearls: Three drug interactions to remember: NSAID + ACE-I + thiazide all increase lithium level by 25-40% (AMH).

stem: A 26-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has had two manic episodes in the past 18 months despite lithium 1000 mg daily (level 0.8 mmol/L, partner-confirmed adherence). She and her partner are planning a pregnancy within the next 12 months and have stopped contraception. She is otherwise well, takes no other medications, has normal renal/hepatic function, normal TSH, normal weight, no PCOS history. Her predominant illness course is MANIC (no significant depressive episodes documented). She has no current depressive symptoms, no psychosis, no current suicide risk and has been adherent with stable lithium troughs. She asks specifically about adding sodium valproate (her sister with epilepsy takes it) and about lamotrigine.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mood, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.8 mmol/L, FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all within reference. Pregnancy test negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in this preconception window?

options.text: Add sodium valproate 500 mg BD as adjunct mood stabiliser to lithium
options.rationale: Adding valproate in woman of reproductive potential who has stopped contraception is contraindicated under TGA Valproate PPP. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

options.text: Add quetiapine extended-release titrated to 300-600 mg nocte with metabolic monitoring, continuing lithium at its current dose
options.rationale: Her predominant pattern is mania despite therapeutic lithium; the evidence base supports atypical antipsychotic adjunct (quetiapine, olanzapine) for breakthrough mania. Quetiapine has a more reassuring preconception profile than valproate, no Pregnancy Prevention Program, and acceptable monitoring requirements. Lithium is retained because her trough is therapeutic and switching would risk further mania during the planning window.

options.text: Switch lithium to carbamazepine 200 mg BD
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is teratogenic (NTDs), induces hepatic enzymes (OCP interactions) and has weaker mania prophylaxis evidence. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Stop lithium immediately because of pregnancy planning
options.rationale: Lithium absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is ~1-2 per 1000 (modest); stopping without replacement risks relapse. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

options.text: Switch lithium to lamotrigine 200 mg daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine has efficacy for bipolar DEPRESSION prophylaxis but is INFERIOR to lithium for mania prophylaxis. Would be correct only in depression-predominant bipolar (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and in any woman of childbearing potential without a Pregnancy Prevention Programme due to major malformation (~10%) and neurodevelopmental impairment (~30-40%) risks. Quetiapine is the first-line alternative in pre-conception planning; lithium can be continued with informed consent; carbamazepine carries neural tube risk; lamotrigine does not prevent mania (RANZCP; TGA).
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: ~10% major malformations, ~30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
Australian TGA restriction: avoid in women of childbearing potential without PPP
Quetiapine first-line atypical in bipolar pregnancy planning
Lamotrigine prevents bipolar DEPRESSION not MANIA
Lithium continuation requires foetal cardiac scan at 18-20 weeks
explanation.pearls: If a question mentions both bipolar AND a woman of reproductive age, valproate is almost always the wrong answer (TGA; RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: TCA overdose - cardiac toxicity and sodium bicarbonate
stem: A 32-year-old woman with chronic depression is brought to ED 90 minutes after deliberate ingestion of approximately 50 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets with alcohol. She is drowsy but rousable (GCS 12), has had a single self-terminating generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival, pupils dilated, mucous membranes dry, catheter drained 200 mL. ECG: sinus tachycardia 132 bpm, QRS 140 ms, terminal R wave in aVR 4 mm, QTc 510 ms. BP 92/54. VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, Na 138, K 3.6. No paracetamol co-ingestion. No other regular medications.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dilated pupils, dry mucosae, post-ictal.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, QRS 140 ms, terminal R in aVR 4 mm, QTc 510 ms. VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, Na 138, K 3.6. Paracetamol level negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention, and what is the threshold guiding it?

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to narrow QRS below ~120 ms and target pH 7.50-7.55; indicated for QRS >100-120 ms, hypotension or seizures
options.rationale: TCA toxicity: bicarbonate reverses cardiac sodium channel blockade by raising serum pH and providing a sodium load. Australian thresholds for bicarbonate (eTG Toxicology, NSW Poisons Information Centre): QRS >100-120 ms, terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, broad-complex arrhythmia, or seizure. She meets multiple. Target QRS <120 ms, pH 7.50-7.55. This is a textbook L3 AU-cutoff application.

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to reverse sedation despite the risk of unmasking seizures in a mixed tricyclic overdose presentation
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed/unknown overdose (precipitates refractory seizures, especially in TCA where seizures already likely). Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg over 20 minutes for the wide-complex tachycardia despite its QT-prolonging and sodium-channel-blocking effects
options.rationale: Class III antiarrhythmics including amiodarone are contraindicated in TCA-induced wide-complex rhythms (further prolong QT, precipitate torsades). Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Gastric lavage and oral activated charcoal 50 g at 90 minutes post-ingestion despite the drowsiness and the unprotected airway risk
options.rationale: Gastric lavage is no longer recommended; charcoal at 90 min has limited benefit and unprotected airway in GCS 12 raises aspiration risk. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: IV physostigmine 1 mg slow push to reverse anticholinergic effects despite the risk of asystole in TCA-mediated cardiotoxicity at this stage
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose (associated with asystole and seizures); anticholinergic features are manifestation of broader toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

explanation.summary: TCA cardiotoxicity is driven by fast sodium channel blockade producing QRS widening, hypotension, and ventricular arrhythmia. Sodium bicarbonate is the targeted therapy: indications are QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, or seizures. Bolus 1-2 mmol/kg, repeat to narrow QRS and target pH 7.50-7.55 (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).
key_points: Bicarbonate indications: QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, seizures
Contraindicated antiarrhythmics: class Ia, Ic, and III (amiodarone, procainamide, flecainide)
Flumazenil contraindicated in mixed TCA ingestions
Physostigmine contraindicated in TCA overdose
Activated charcoal within 1-2 hours only if alert with protected airway
explanation.pearls: Terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm is the most specific bedside marker of TCA cardiotoxicity (eTG).

stem: A 41-year-old woman who has been on venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily for 14 months for MDD agrees with her GP to reduce her dose. Within 48 hours of dropping to 75 mg daily she develops brief electric-shock sensations ('brain zaps'), dizziness, nausea, headache, insomnia and intermittent crying spells with irritability. PHQ-9 four days earlier on 150 mg was 4. Her mood remains reactive, she still enjoys her usual activities, has no anhedonia, no early-morning waking, no suicidal ideation, no diurnal mood variation. She is still functioning at work and socially engaged. Symptoms began within 48 hours of the dose reduction. She has no other new medications, no infections, no thyroid symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Reactive affect, mild irritability, no anhedonia, no psychomotor change, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 at 150 mg dose 4 days ago: score 4.
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine 75 mg daily (reduced from 150 mg 48 hours ago).

lead_in: Which is the single most likely cause of her symptoms, and what is the most appropriate immediate response?

options.text: Recurrence of major depressive disorder; reinstate full venlafaxine dose and refer urgently for psychiatric review and ECT consideration today
options.rationale: Relapse of MDD typically develops over weeks not 48 h; includes core symptoms (low mood, anhedonia, neurovegetative) absent here. Would be correct with classic depressive features (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome; resume venlafaxine 150 mg and taper more slowly (37.5 mg reductions every 2-4 weeks); venlafaxine's short half-life predisposes
options.rationale: Discontinuation syndrome: onset within days (typically 2-5 days) of dose reduction or cessation, FINISH mnemonic (Flu-like, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbance including brain zaps, Hyperarousal). Venlafaxine is among the highest-risk antidepressants due to short half-life (~5 hours parent, ~11 hours active metabolite). Management: reinstate previous dose, then taper more slowly. This is the AU-cutoff: venlafaxine taper steps should be smaller (37.5 mg) and more spaced than SSRIs.

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from a drug interaction; cease all serotonergic agents and commence cyproheptadine with active supportive cooling measures
options.rationale: Serotonin syndrome features hyperreflexia, clonus, hyperthermia and follows ADDITION of serotonergic agent, not dose REDUCTION. Would be correct with serotonergic addition (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Hypothyroidism from long-term SNRI use; commence thyroxine replacement and recheck TSH at 6 weeks before considering any further taper of venlafaxine
options.rationale: SNRIs do not cause hypothyroidism; no thyroid symptoms and no temporal link with dose change. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Serotonin withdrawal-induced generalised anxiety disorder; commence sertraline as an alternative SSRI with cognitive behavioural therapy referral today
options.rationale: GAD requires 6+ months of pervasive worry with characteristic features; syndrome here is acute, sensorimotor and time-locked to dose reduction. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Discontinuation syndrome (FINISH) develops within days of antidepressant dose reduction, is short-half-life-drug-prone (paroxetine, venlafaxine, duloxetine), lasts 1-2 weeks, and resolves on dose restoration. Relapse develops gradually over weeks with anhedonia and progressive functional decline (AMH; RANZCP).
key_points: FINISH: Flu-like, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbance (brain zaps), Hyperarousal
Onset within days, resolves within 1-2 weeks or immediately on dose restoration
Highest-risk agents: venlafaxine, paroxetine, duloxetine (short half-life)
Lowest-risk: fluoxetine (long half-life serves as self-taper)
Distinguish from relapse: rapid onset, sensory features, retained reactivity
explanation.pearls: Resolution within 24-48 hours of dose restoration confirms discontinuation rather than relapse (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Chronic vs acute risk factors and safety planning
stem: A 38-year-old Aboriginal man presents to ED after an impulsive paracetamol ingestion of 8 tablets following an argument with his partner. He is medically cleared (no transaminitis, paracetamol level below the Rumack-Matthew nomogram line). History: one previous self-harm 4 years ago; harmful alcohol use (8 standard drinks/day currently); unemployment 12 months; recent eviction. He has chronic back pain and stopped his SSRI 3 months ago because he 'could not afford it'. Tonight he says the argument was 'the last straw' and although he does not want to die now, he feels 'hopeless about anything getting better'. He has a legally-licensed firearm at home for pest control. His brother completed suicide 8 years ago. He has not yet been linked with the local Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Service. He agrees to attend follow-up if it is arranged for him.

data_table.Examination: Hopeless, mildly intoxicated, no psychosis, no agitation, no suicide plan at this moment.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level non-toxic. LFTs normal. UEC normal. BAC 0.18%.

lead_in: Of the listed factors, which is the single most important MODIFIABLE acute risk factor to address before discharge planning under current Australian suicide-prevention guidance?

options.text: Brother's completed suicide 8 years ago
options.rationale: Family history is non-modifiable distal risk factor; cannot be acted on to reduce acute risk. Would never be the modifiable answer (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: His Aboriginal identity as a demographic factor
options.rationale: Identity is not a risk factor to be 'modified'; culturally-safe care and ACCHS linkage are the appropriate response, not pathologising identity. Would never be correct (Source: NACCHO).
source_refs: National Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Suicide Prevention Strategy

options.text: Previous self-harm episode 4 years ago
options.rationale: Past self-harm is strong non-modifiable historical risk factor; cannot be addressed acutely to change risk. Would never be the modifiable answer (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Pervasive hopelessness about the future improving
options.rationale: Hopelessness is strongest psychological correlate of suicide and modifiable through therapy and treating mood disorder but in the discharge-window timeframe it cannot be eliminated; means restriction is the immediately modifiable factor (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Access to a firearm at home (means restriction)
options.rationale: Means restriction is the highest-evidence acute modifiable intervention in suicide prevention: removing or securing the firearm tonight (e.g. via partner-removal, police safe-storage, or temporary surrender) reduces lethality even if other risk factors persist. The acute window after presentation is when access to lethal means most strongly predicts completed suicide. RANZCP, Black Dog Institute and the National Suicide Prevention Strategy all rank means restriction as the most actionable acute step. (Resuming SSRI access and ACCHS linkage are also critical, but means restriction is rate-limiting.)

explanation.summary: Suicide risk is structured into static (chronic, non-modifiable: previous attempt, family history, male sex, mental illness) and dynamic (acute, modifiable: hopelessness, recent stressors, intoxication, means access, lack of social connection). Means restriction is the single most evidence-based modifiable acute factor in Australian and international guidelines (RANZCP; LIFE Framework).
key_points: Chronic non-modifiable: prior attempt, family history of suicide, male sex, chronic illness, prior trauma
Acute modifiable: means access, intoxication, recent stressors, hopelessness, lack of supports
Means restriction = highest-impact acute modifiable intervention
Safety plan: warning signs, internal coping, social distractors, supports, professional help, means restriction
Aboriginal mental health care: link with ACCHS for culturally safe follow-up
explanation.pearls: If a question lists access to a firearm or large medication stockpile, that is the modifiable factor to prioritise (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

subtopic_detail: Safety plan after self-harm (DBT chain analysis)
stem: A 23-year-old woman with established borderline personality disorder presents to ED after superficial wrist cutting following a perceived rejection by a friend. She has had 6 similar presentations over 12 months. She is medically cleared after wound closure and tetanus update. She has an established treatment relationship with a community DBT-informed team; her case manager is contactable and has texted the ED to confirm she is engaged with treatment. She declines admission. She is no longer acutely distressed and denies current suicidal intent. She agrees that she 'just snapped' when she saw the unread message. She has no alcohol or substances on board tonight. She lives with her supportive mother who is present in ED. She asks for help with what to do differently next time.

data_table.Examination: Calm, reactive affect, no current suicidal ideation, no psychosis, superficial laceration sutured.
data_table.Investigations: Wounds superficial, tetanus updated, paracetamol level negative, BAC zero.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step before discharge, per the National Health and Medical Research Council Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder?

options.text: Admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act for inpatient stabilisation despite her ongoing engagement with the outpatient DBT programme today
options.rationale: Routine admission of BPD self-harm without acute suicide intent or psychosis worsens long-term outcomes (regression, role-reinforcement, iatrogenic harm). Would be correct only with acute suicide intent or psychosis (Source: NHMRC BPD).
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder 2013

options.text: Discharge with a 'no-suicide contract' signed by the patient and her mother without formal safety planning or any specific follow-up arrangement
options.rationale: 'No-suicide contracts' have NO evidence for reducing suicide risk and may worsen outcomes by substituting paper artefact for genuine engagement. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Collaboratively develop a Stanley-Brown style safety plan (warning signs, coping, social support, means restriction), share with DBT team, arrange same-week DBT contact
options.rationale: NHMRC BPD Guideline and the National Suicide Prevention Strategy endorse Stanley-Brown safety planning as the evidence-based ED discharge tool. The six steps include explicit means restriction and graded coping/social/professional escalation. Sharing the plan with her established DBT team preserves continuity of care, which is the active ingredient in BPD outcomes.

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg daily for affective instability and discharge home with primary care follow-up arranged in 2 weeks for medication review
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for BPD; medications target specific comorbidities not the disorder itself. Would be correct only for documented comorbid depression/psychosis (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Refer to drug and alcohol service for substance-use treatment despite the absence of any current substance misuse history in this presentation
options.rationale: No alcohol or substances on board tonight and no documented substance-use comorbidity; mis-referral pathologises non-existent problem. Would be correct only with documented substance comorbidity (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: After a self-harm presentation, collaborative safety planning (Stanley-Brown) combined with continuity of psychological care is evidence-based and reduces repeat suicidal behaviour. DBT chain analysis at the next session unpacks the precipitating chain. Involuntary admission, no-suicide contracts, and ED-initiated pharmacotherapy are not first-line (RANZCP; LIFE Framework).
key_points: Stanley-Brown Safety Plan: 6 components, collaboratively developed, written copy with patient
Continuity > containment: handover to established team is critical
No-suicide contracts have no evidence and may be harmful
BPD first-line treatment is structured psychotherapy (DBT, MBT, schema)
Chain analysis identifies vulnerabilities, precipitants, behaviour, consequences for skills practice
explanation.pearls: If a question describes superficial self-harm in a person with BPD and intact supports, safety plan + community handover beats admission (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

stem: A 68-year-old man is reviewed in stroke clinic 8 weeks after a moderate left middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke. He has residual mild right-sided weakness and mild expressive dysphasia from which he is recovering. His wife reports that since week 3, he has been progressively withdrawn, has stopped attending physiotherapy sessions, cries during morning routines, has lost 4 kg, sleeps 9 hours but wakes unrefreshed, and says repeatedly 'I'm a burden'. His PHQ-9 today is 17 (excluding his neurological items). He has no prior psychiatric history. His current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and a multivitamin. He has no suicidal plan but admits passive thoughts that 'it would be easier if I didn't wake up'. There is no evidence of vascular depression alone explaining the pattern, and no apathy syndrome without mood symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Mild right hemiparesis (improving), mild expressive dysphasia, low affect, congruent, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 17. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, folate all within reference.
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, multivitamin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Sertraline 25 mg daily titrated to 50 mg, plus structured psychotherapy and rehabilitation engagement
options.rationale: Post-stroke depression affects approximately 1 in 3 stroke survivors, peaks at 3-6 months, and meaningfully impairs rehabilitation outcomes. SSRI treatment (sertraline or citalopram are the preferred Australian first-line options for cardiovascular comorbidity) plus active rehabilitation engagement improves both mood and functional outcome. Combination beats either modality alone (Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines; RANZCP).
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Reassure that low mood is normal after stroke and review in 3 months
options.rationale: PHQ-9 17 reflects moderate-to-severe depression with functional impact (refusing rehab, passive death thoughts); delaying treatment 3 months risks worse outcomes. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Start mirtazapine 30 mg nocte plus low-dose amitriptyline 25 mg for sleep and mood
options.rationale: Amitriptyline (TCA) has significant anticholinergic, orthostatic, cardiac risks in older post-stroke patients; sertraline or citalopram preferred. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Refer urgently for ECT as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: ECT is for severe depression with catatonia, food/fluid refusal, high suicide risk, or treatment resistance; not first-line in moderate post-stroke depression. Would be correct in severe/treatment-resistant (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg BD orally for anxiety symptoms
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines in older adults increase falls, cognitive impairment and second stroke risk; not appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Post-stroke depression affects ~30% of stroke survivors, peaks at 3-6 months, and worsens rehabilitation outcomes. Stroke Foundation Australia endorses SSRI plus active rehabilitation engagement as first-line. Sertraline or citalopram are preferred due to cardiovascular safety profile (Stroke Foundation; RANZCP).
key_points: PSD prevalence ~30%, peak 3-6 months
Australian first-line SSRI: sertraline or citalopram
Avoid TCAs in older post-stroke patients
Combine pharmacotherapy with active rehabilitation engagement
PHQ-9 may overestimate due to overlap with stroke-related items - interpret carefully
explanation.pearls: Untreated PSD worsens not just mood but motor and cognitive recovery (Stroke Foundation Australia).

sources.label: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

subtopic_detail: Postpartum mood disorder after pregnancy loss
stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her GP 6 weeks after a stillbirth at 32 weeks gestation. She reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, insomnia with early morning waking, weight loss of 3 kg, feelings of worthlessness extending beyond bereavement (she believes she is 'fundamentally damaged'), and intermittent passive thoughts of being better off not being here. She has not returned to work and avoids contact with friends who have babies. No psychotic symptoms, no delusions or hallucinations. EPDS today is 18 with item 10 (self-harm) scored 1 (occasionally has the thought). No prior psychiatric history. She is not breastfeeding (no surviving infant). She would like to try again in the future. She lives with a supportive partner.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, congruent affect, no psychosis, no thought disorder.
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 18, item 10 score 1. TSH 1.6, FBC, UEC, LFTs, B12, ferritin all within reference.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan under the COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guideline and RANZCP CPG?

options.text: Watchful waiting for grief resolution over 3-6 months with no specific treatment
options.rationale: Her presentation goes beyond uncomplicated grief: EPDS 18 with item 10 positive, neurovegetative features, anhedonia, worthlessness, 6 weeks of impairment. Would be correct in uncomplicated grief (Source: COPE).

options.text: Diagnose prolonged grief disorder and offer grief therapy alone
options.rationale: Prolonged grief disorder requires symptoms at least 12 months after loss in adults; she is at 6 weeks. Would be correct at 12+ months (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Start a benzodiazepine for short-term anxiolysis and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying in MDD or grief, risk dependence, and may impair cognitive grief processing. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

options.text: Sertraline 25-50 mg daily titrated as tolerated, plus structured psychotherapy (CBT or IPT) with a perinatal-experienced clinician, plus grief-informed peer support (e.g. SANDS Australia), with weekly GP review until stable
options.rationale: Combined antidepressant plus psychotherapy is recommended for moderate-to-severe perinatal MDD with item 10 positive on EPDS. Sertraline is first-line SSRI per COPE (her potential to breastfeed in future pregnancies makes sertraline a sensible default choice). Perinatal-experienced psychological therapy plus grief-informed peer support (SANDS Australia specifically supports stillbirth/neonatal loss) addresses both the MDE and the grief processes. Frequent GP review covers safety.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline 2023

options.text: Recommend immediate next pregnancy as therapeutic to resolve mood symptoms
options.rationale: Replacement child framing is contrary to perinatal bereavement guidance and risks complicated grief and PTSD in subsequent pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

explanation.summary: Perinatal mental health screening (EPDS, score >=13 prompts assessment) after pregnancy loss identifies treatable depression that should not be dismissed as 'normal grief'. COPE 2023 endorses combined SSRI plus structured psychotherapy with grief-informed peer support. Sertraline is the preferred Australian SSRI in perinatal contexts (COPE; RANZCP).
key_points: EPDS >=13 = positive screen; item 10 always reviewed for self-harm
Grief vs depression: grief is undulating; depression is pervasive with worthlessness and neurovegetative features
Sertraline is first-line Australian perinatal SSRI
SANDS and Red Nose are Australian bereavement supports
Prolonged grief disorder requires >=12 months in adults
explanation.pearls: After stillbirth, ask about EVERY pregnancy loss in subsequent years - anniversary reactions are common (COPE).

sources.label: COPE: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period - Australian Clinical Practice Guideline

subtopic_detail: Adolescent suicide risk - specific risk factors
stem: A 16-year-old young person is brought to ED by their mother after she found drafts of a goodbye note on their bedroom desk. The young person reluctantly discloses they have been experiencing low mood for 4 months, having previously been a strong student now failing assessments. HEEADSSS interview reveals: school refusal beginning when they came out as transgender to peers and experienced 6 weeks of escalating online abuse, current alcohol use 2-3 nights per week with peers, no current medications, broken sleep, and a friend who completed suicide 5 months ago. They feel rejected by their father since coming out. Their mother is supportive but exhausted. They acknowledge a specific plan involving paracetamol stockpiled at home, with intended timing this weekend when their mother is travelling. There is no current psychosis. They have no current GP and are not engaged with any mental health service.

data_table.Examination: Withdrawn, restricted affect, no psychosis, fully oriented.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level negative on arrival. UEC, LFTs, FBC within reference.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with mother, written safety plan, and outpatient youth mental health referral
options.rationale: Specific plan, available means at home, intended timing within 48 h with unsupervised window, recent bereavement by suicide, escalating bullying - discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Admit (voluntary or involuntary) to adolescent mental health unit and link with LGBTQAI+ youth supports
options.rationale: Multiple acute high-risk features (specific plan + means + timing + recent peer suicide + active bullying + alienation from one parent + substance use + lack of professional supports) require an inpatient level of safety while initial assessment, family work, and treatment are established. Australian Mental Health Acts permit involuntary admission of minors when criteria are met. Affirming the young person's identity and connecting with LGBTQAI+ youth services (QLife, Twenty10, Minus18) is integral to care - gender minority youth suicide risk is elevated by family rejection and reduced by family acceptance, school connectedness, and culturally safe care (headspace; RANZCP; Trans Pathways).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 10 mg daily tonight in ED and discharge home with parent
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is first-line for adolescent MDD but starting in ED with discharge into high acute-risk environment does not address immediate risk. Would be correct as part of inpatient plan (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).

options.text: Notify child protection services and remove the young person from the home
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting applies where significant harm risk from a parent; mother is supportive and immediate risk is to self, not parental. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MRG).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance

options.text: Reassure as 'normal teenage stress' and arrange routine GP follow-up next week
options.rationale: Specific plan, means, timing, recent peer suicide, active bullying are one of the highest-acuity adolescent presentations; minimising is dangerous. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance

explanation.summary: Adolescent-specific suicide risk factors include peer suicide bereavement (contagion), bullying (especially cyberbullying), gender and sexuality minority stress, family rejection, substance use, and impulsivity. The Australian Trans Pathways study documented dramatically elevated self-harm in transgender youth, largely modifiable by family acceptance, peer connectedness, and access to gender-affirming care. Multiple acute features mandate inpatient safety in this presentation (headspace; RANZCP; Trans Pathways).
key_points: HEEADSSS structures adolescent psychosocial screening
Adolescent-specific risks: peer suicide, bullying, identity-related stress, family rejection
LGBTQAI+ supports: QLife (1800 184 527), Twenty10, Minus18, headspace
Family acceptance is a strong protective factor for transgender youth
Mental Health Acts permit involuntary admission of minors when criteria met
explanation.pearls: Means restriction at home + LGBTQAI+ affirming care are two of the highest-yield modifiable interventions in transgender youth suicide prevention (Trans Pathways).

sources.label: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

subtopic_detail: B12 deficiency presenting as cognitive and mood symptoms
stem: A 72-year-old woman is referred to community psychiatry by her general practitioner with a 5-month history of progressive low mood, irritability, paranoid ideas about her neighbour, episodes of confusion, and worsening short-term memory. She has lost 6 kg, has reduced appetite, and complains of intermittent paraesthesia in both feet and unsteady gait, particularly in the dark. She has fallen twice in 3 months. Past medical history includes autoimmune thyroiditis on stable levothyroxine (TSH 1.8 today) and 12 months of pantoprazole 40 mg daily for reflux. She has been a strict vegan for 25 years and takes no vitamin supplements. She drinks no alcohol. On examination she has glossitis with a beefy tongue, mild distal sensory loss to vibration and proprioception in both feet, brisk knee reflexes with absent ankle reflexes, Romberg positive, and MoCA 19 of 30. Skin is pale with a lemon-yellow tint. FBC and film: Hb 95 g/L, MCV 112 fL, hypersegmented neutrophils on film.

data_table.Examination: Glossitis, lemon-yellow pallor, reduced vibration and proprioception in both feet, brisk knee reflexes, absent ankle reflexes, Romberg positive, MoCA 19/30.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 95 g/L (low), MCV 112 fL (high), hypersegmented neutrophils on film. TSH 1.8. UEC normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Serum B12, folate, ferritin, methylmalonic acid, homocysteine, and intrinsic factor antibodies
options.rationale: Macrocytic anaemia (MCV 112) with hypersegmented neutrophils, glossitis, dorsal column signs (vibration/proprioception loss, Romberg positive), brisk knee + absent ankle reflexes (subacute combined degeneration pattern), cognitive impairment, mood and paranoid features in a long-term vegan ALSO on chronic PPI therapy is a textbook B12 deficiency. Australian first-line workup is serum B12 + folate; methylmalonic acid and homocysteine confirm intracellular deficiency when B12 is borderline. Intrinsic factor antibodies identify pernicious anaemia. Hypothyroidism is already excluded by current TSH (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; AMH).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Brain MRI for suspected vascular dementia and silent infarcts
options.rationale: Vascular dementia can cause cognitive/mood symptoms but does NOT cause macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, hypersegmented neutrophils, or selective dorsal column signs. Would be correct in pure vascular cognitive impairment (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for autoimmune encephalitis antibody workup
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis presents sub-acutely with psychiatric symptoms plus seizures, movement disorder, autonomic instability; macrocytic anaemia and dorsal column signs do not fit. Would be correct with autoimmune encephalitis features (Source: Titulaer 2013).

options.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 to rule out hypothyroid relapse
options.rationale: TSH 1.8 within reference; hypothyroidism does not produce macrocytosis of this degree, hypersegmented neutrophils, glossitis, or dorsal column signs. Would be correct with biochemically active hypothyroidism (Source: APEG).

options.text: CT abdomen for occult malignancy as cause of weight loss
options.rationale: Weight loss has plausible nutritional explanation (long-term veganism, B12 deficiency, glossitis impairing eating); occult malignancy workup is reasonable later if B12 replacement does not resolve. Would be correct after B12 trial without response (Source: RACGP).

explanation.summary: B12 deficiency is a high-yield psychiatric mimic in older adults, vegans, and those on chronic acid suppression. The diagnostic fingerprint integrates macrocytic anaemia + hypersegmented neutrophils + glossitis + selective dorsal column signs (subacute combined degeneration) + cognitive and mood symptoms. Australian workup pairs B12 with folate, ferritin, and confirmatory MMA/homocysteine; intrinsic factor antibodies define pernicious anaemia (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; AMH).
key_points: High-risk groups: older adults, vegans, chronic PPI/H2RA, metformin, post-gastrectomy/bariatric, ileal disease
Subacute combined degeneration: dorsal columns (vibration, proprioception) + corticospinal tracts (brisk knee, absent ankle reflexes, Babinski)
Workup: B12, folate, ferritin; MMA and homocysteine if borderline; intrinsic factor antibodies for PA
Replacement: IM hydroxocobalamin 1 mg alternate days for 2 weeks then 3-monthly (PA) or as required
Mood, psychosis, and cognitive symptoms can be the presenting feature - check B12 in any new geriatric mood or cognitive presentation
explanation.pearls: Brisk knee reflex WITH absent ankle reflex is the classic mixed-tract sign of subacute combined degeneration (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview
